data.title: Western Alliance: Solid Q4 With Growth Momentum Building (Upgrade)
data.description:
Shares of Western Alliance Bancorporation (WAL) have been a poor performer over the past year, losing 7% and missing out on a market rally. While the company has generated strong deposit growth, investors have been cautious about potential credit losses, given its fairly light reserves and exposure to private credit, which caused some losses in Q3. In July, I rated Western Alliance a “Hold” given a cheap valuation but concerns about the adequacy of reserves. Since then, shares have gained 6%, lagging the market’s 10% rally. I view this positive but market-lagging return as being consistent with the rating. With updated financials, now is a good time to revisit WAL. I am more bullish now.

In the company’s fourth quarter, Western Alliance earned $2.59 per share, which blew past estimates by $0.22. For the full year, WAL earned $8.73. For comparison, I was targeting $8.20-$8.60 back in July, as credit costs were better than feared and balance sheet growth remains strong. After facing significant pressure during the 2023 regional banking crisis, Western Alliance has rebuilt momentum in its business, showcasing its significant earnings power and returning to growth.

Balance sheet growth is strong

Looking first at its balance sheet, deposits grew by $11 billion, or 16%, from a year ago to $77 billion. Balances were down $88 million in Q4, consistent with seasonal outflows. Given its mortgage warehousing business, Western Alliance has a high share of valuable, noninterest-bearing deposits (“NIB”). These balances tend to seasonally decline in Q4, but they were up nearly $6 billion from last year, which is supportive of margins. After facing significant pressure in 2022-2024, these balances have clearly returned to growth. Interest-bearing deposit costs were down a solid 25bps sequentially, reflecting pass-through of Fed rate cuts.

During the quarter, Western Alliance added $2 billion of loans, bringing its portfolio to $58.7 billion. Loans were up 9% from last year, and with deposit growth outpacing loan growth, its loan-to-deposit ratio fell by 490bps to 76%, a healthy level, leaving Western Alliance with substantial liquidity. All of its loan growth in Q4 was tied to business lending, and compared to most regional banks, its commercial real estate exposure is fairly low at about 18%.

Within its CRE portfolio, Western Alliance has conservative underwriting with loans generally in the 50-60% loan-to-value, providing substantial equity cushions and minimizing default risk. About $2 billion of its loans are to the office sector. These are primarily bridge loans, and the majority mature this year. Its LTVs are also fairly conservative, and I do not expect significant losses from this exposure.

Thanks to balance sheet growth, net interest income (“NII”) was up 15% from last year to $766 million. In Q4, WAL’s net interest margin (“NIM”) came in at 3.51%, down 2bps from Q4 given the impact of Fed rate cuts. NIM faces some seasonal Q4 pressure as NIB balances decline, but NIM was up 3bps from last year. This reflects strong pull-through of Fed rate cuts on deposits as well as growing NIB balances, which have helped to offset lower floating rate yields on loans during this cutting cycle. I expect NIM to remain around 3.5% in 2026.

Noninterest income was particularly strong at $215 million vs. $188 million in Q3, driving much of the EPS beat. The company generated a $16 million net gain on its mortgage loan origination and sales activity. This income can be volatile quarter to quarter. While volumes were down 2% to $15 billion, its gain on sale increased to 30bps from 27bps. With interest rates having ticked down a bit and President Trump pushing Fannie and Freddie to increase their holdings, this should support higher volumes and margins in this business going forward.

Low reserves are offset by solid capital

Turning to credit quality, the company took 31bps of charge-offs, up 9bps from Q3. Nonperforming loans were down 7bps sequentially to 85bps. During the quarter, WAL set aside $73 million in reserves for credit losses, adding a net ~$30 million to reserves, given loan growth. The company now has ~$510 million in reserves. I continue to believe that reserves are too low and are the primary risk to a bull case. While WAL has been forced to add over $70 million to reserves in Q2, its allowance to NPL ratio is just 1x. I like to see this metric at 1.5-2.5x. As such, I expect continued reserve builds, which could be over $250 million if we were to see the economy weaken.

This weak reserve position is offset by the fact that Western Alliance maintains a solid balance sheet with a tier 1 common equity capital ratio (CET1) of 11.0%. This was down 30bps from last year, given the significant growth in its balance sheet. WAL has a relatively modest $344 million loss in AOCI, and even factoring this in, capital would be above 10.5%, a healthy level. During the quarter, WAL inched back into repurchases, buying back $58 million of stock. Thanks to these, its share count is down about 0.5% from last year. Additionally, shares offer a 1.9% dividend yield. While capital is strong, I expect the pace of buybacks to remain fairly muted (though likely higher than Q4) as WAL uses its excess capital posture to support ongoing balance sheet growth.

Strong balance sheet growth can lift shares

Alongside results, management provided guidance for 2026. As you can see below, the company plans for ongoing balance sheet growth with loans and deposits each up about 10%. This is fairly consistent with recent trends, and I also expect the macro environment for loan growth to be easier given somewhat lower rates. Assuming two rate cuts, I expect a fairly stable NIM. Management does expect a tick-up in charge-offs, given higher NPLs. This, combined with the need to add reserves, means we should see at least $150 million more in credit costs in 2026 than in 2025.

Given this strong balance sheet growth, Western Alliance should generate at least $11 of 2026 EPS, even with higher credit costs. That leaves shares at about 8x earnings. Looking at another valuation metric, tangible book value ended the quarter at $61.29, so shares are below 1.5x book value, whereas many regional banks are approaching 12x. WAL’s history of more volatile deposits and its relatively low reserves limit valuation, though I view its excess capital and low reserves as essentially being offsetting factors.

Nonetheless, some valuation discount is merited and likely to persist. However, this discount has become extreme. I believe shares should trade closer to 10x, and I expect shares to at least break past $100 over the next year with upside as high as $110, especially as its mortgage banking operation should generate stronger results this year than last year. As a result, I am upgrading shares to "Buy" and view the value opportunity in WAL as compelling.

data.title: Leidos: Defense Player Moving Into Power
data.description:
Shares of Leidos Holdings, Inc. (LDOS) have been an active participant in the 2025 rally in defense stocks. After a solid, but not too inspiring, performance last year, the company started 2026 with an interesting and strategic acquisition, one that gets my approval. While the deal will add some near-term leverage and rather insignificant revenues and earnings, I like the direction of travel.

After a strong share price performance, shares still trade at very reasonable valuation multiples, as Leidos has never commanded premium valuations. I am interested, but not running to chase the shares here, looking to provide further updates as the year progresses and I get more familiar with the business.

Defense

Leidos describes itself as a global science & technology business that provides services like IT, engineering, and science solutions used in defense, intelligence, civil, and health markets. Having seen a rich corporate history, Leidos has grown to more than a $16 billion revenue base in 2024.

These revenues are split across no less than four categories, the largest of which is a +$7 billion national security & digital business. This is complemented by a rapidly growing and very profitable $5 billion health & civil business. These two core segments are complemented by a +$2 billion commercial & international and +$2 billion defense systems business.

The company has come a long way over the past decade, with revenues having tripled, in part due to a 2016 merger with Lockheed Martin's Information Systems business. The company has seen operating margins rise from mid-single digits to low-double digits, yet the share count on a net basis has risen some 70% as well, marking a significant increase.

Nonetheless, investors have seen handsome returns as a $60 stock in 2015, advanced to the $100 mark around the time of the pandemic, only to see strong momentum in 2024. Currently, shares are trading at $190 per share, after recently trading in the low $200s.

2024 As A Base

Still awaiting the release of the 2025 results, I start by going back to early 2025 when Leidos reported resilient returns for 2024, with revenues up nearly 8% to $16.7 billion. GAAP operating profits of $1.83 billion look quite clean with GAAP earnings reported at $9.22 per share. Adjusted earnings advanced in a spectacular fashion, reported about a dollar higher at $10.21 per share. Most of this discrepancy relates to amortization charges, making me comfortable to use the adjusted earnings numbers here.

Net debt is reported at $3.7 billion, which is no issue at all with adjusted EBITDA up to $2.1 billion. Furthermore, following an impressive 1.4 times book-to-bill-ratio, a backlog exceeding $43 billion is equal to about 2.5 times sales.

This rapid increase in the backlog was not seen converting into 2025. The company only guided for 2025 sales up to $16.9-$17.3 billion, marking very modest sales gains anticipated. Adjusted earnings are seen around $10.55 per share, plus or minus twenty cents. This comes in part as the company is cautious following the DOGE movement, at that time still being in full swing.

Trading around $140 at the time, multiples were rather modest in all fairness, although Leidos has never really commanded too much of a premium valuation. While Leidos can be considered a defense business, much of its solutions are services instead of real products, making them more vulnerable to DOGE concerns, still very much alive at the time.

Delivering

After solid first-quarter earnings were reported, the company maintained the full-year guidance, but this felt conservative. This certainly was the case when the company announced a $300 million deal to acquire Kudu Dynamics in May to deliver on AI-enabled cyber capabilities for its customers.

Following resilient reports, the company hiked the full-year guidance in both the second and third quarters. By now, adjusted earnings are seen at about $11.60 per share, plus or minus fifteen cents, all based on revenues between $17.00 and $17.25 billion, indicating that the gains are more seen in margins than topline sales. Meanwhile, the company adjusted its portfolio further with a bolt-on divestment of Varec.

The company has seen continued gains in the backlog, which exceeded $47 billion; this number is up about $2 billion on a 12-month basis, although much of this is an unfunded backlog, of course. Net debt was pretty stable at $3.7 billion.

By now, the 130 million shares trade at $190, for a near $25 billion equity valuation and just over $28 billion enterprise valuation. This values the business at about 1.6 times sales, and with EBITDA trending at $2.4 billion, this comes in at 11–12 times this metric. Based on the adjusted earnings metrics, shares traded at a very reasonable 16-17 times earnings. On top of organic growth, Leidos delivers on continued buybacks, as a less than 1% dividend yield is not too compelling here.

Of course, definitive 2025 results are scheduled for early February, yet with adjusted earnings totaling $9.25 per share for the first nine months of the year, the fourth quarter is typically a bit softer. The real question for investors, besides action on the M&A front, is how quick and to which degree recent order intakes, and growing backlogs, will convert into topline sales growth.

A Substantial Deal

At the start of 2026, the company started the year with somewhat of an interesting deal. Leidos has reached a $2.4 billion deal to acquire ENTRUST Solutions Group from Kohlberg. This adds power design capabilities to its portfolio to bolster its energy infrastructure portfolio.

The acquisition will add engineering expertise to transmission and distribution across the power spectrum, covering both gas and electrical utilities. The deal would double the $600 million energy infrastructure engineering business. The deal is expected to be accretive to revenue growth and EBITDA margins while being accretive to 2027 earnings per share.

That does not really say much, but fortunately the deal presentation reveals a $650 million sales contribution, these revenues growing at a pace in the mid-teens, with adjusted EBITDA margins seen in the low twenties. This suggests at least $130 million in EBITDA contribution, which would be sufficient to offset the additional interest costs incurred here. Pro forma net debt is seen north of $6 billion, for a leverage ratio in the mid-2s.

What Now?

Frankly, I like the move from Leidos, adding commercial exposure to a rapidly growing industry, at somewhat higher valuations, but all this is manageable here. Overall valuations are still relatively modest at 16–17 times, as now some debt will be incurred, but that is nothing too concerning.

In between all this, I am excited to learn about Leidos here. While shares trade at very reasonable valuations, quite a few expectations have been built into the shares, as shares have never commanded a premium valuation in the past, as it appears. This comes as the reliance on the US as key customer and the high-degree of non-product solutions create some uncertainty, but frankly, Leidos has been a solid operator and value creator.

I like the business and like that the deal for ENTRUST introduced me to the firm, making me keen to provide updates on the investment case further into 2026.

data.title: Booz Allen Hamilton: Hold Rating, Buy On Weakness
data.description:
Thesis

Booz Allen Hamilton Holding Corporation (BAH) is one of the United States’ longest-operating government consulting and technology companies dating back to 1914.

Over the past year, BAH shares have lagged the S&P 500 (SP500). And yesterday's negative market reaction, combined with a fresh earnings report, didn't do them any extra favors, bringing an unwanted dose of reality after the Department of the Treasury ended contracts tied to IRS work after deciding that data protections were not sufficient. The trigger was a breach involving a former employee that exposed sensitive taxpayer data. It's not a great situation, to say the least, and it lands with extra weight. Risk tolerance is lower. Expectations are sharper.

After absorbing the data, I’m taking a neutral stance on the stock considering that delayed funding, tighter scrutiny, and stop start award activity limit upside visibility for now.

Revenue Impact: Timing & Government Shutdown

  • For the quarter, gross revenue totaled $2.6 billion, representing a roughly 10% decline versus the prior year period and a 7% decline on a revenue ex-billable basis.
  • We believe, based on our estimates, that these will have a cumulative impact of about $50 million on revenue and $20 million on profit for the full fiscal year.
  • The overall pace of funding was meaningfully slower than prior third quarters, down 32% year-over-year. As a result, funded backlog fell 10% year-over-year.

So this wasn’t a great quarter, but it also wasn’t a collapse. We can see that revenue fell compared to last year, largely because of timing issues. Also, the extended government shutdown didn't help. Naturally, it led to funding decisions being delayed, slowed down contract awards, and made sales cycles longer than desired in an already, let's say, uneven economic environment. Deals didn’t disappear, they just didn’t move. And when your biggest customer hits pause, the stock market lets you feel it.

Civil segment pressure and improving pipeline

  • As anticipated, our Civil business declined about 28% year-over-year.
  • Despite friction in the funding environment, we ended the calendar year with a record year-end backlog of over $38 billion, up about 2% over the prior year.
  • As of December 31, our qualified pipeline for the next fiscal year, that is fiscal year 2027, stands at nearly $53 billion.

The civil segment felt that pain the most. Basically, when the administration changed, agencies had to hit reset. And it really doesn't take much to guess where the funds went as they siphoned money away from long-running programs and transferred toward data readiness and AI platforms. It wasn’t all bad news though. The pipeline is starting to improve. CEO Rozanski provided context, noting that,

“The pipeline is up double digits, both in national security and in civil and we're beginning to see some movement on the award activity on the civil side, which we have not seen all year.”

Margins and cost actions

  • We also completed meaningful actions to adjust our cost structure, dropping our run rate spend by approximately $150 million.
  • Adjusted EBITDA for the third quarter was $285 million.
  • This translated to an adjusted EBITDA margin of 10.9%.

Margins were also one of the bright spots this quarter. Management acted early by cutting costs and reducing overhead before uncertainty could build up, which gave the company some breathing room. However, I think there's an obvious trade-off that comes with that approach. Leaner operations demand a tight ship. And in a stop-start funding environment, any lag in ramping new work could quickly show up in utilization.

  • We expect this to provide $0.47 of incremental benefit to ADEPS for the full fiscal year.

Profitability also got a boost from below-the-line levers. According to CFO Calderone,

"These changes included a higher R&D tax credit from more qualified technical work in our portfolio, as well as additional revenues qualifying for the foreign-derived intangible income deduction."

Some of that benefit should stick, giving Booz Allen more capacity to fund innovation without leaning harder on the cost base.

  • As a result of particularly strong collections in December, free cash flow for the quarter was $248 million, inclusive of $261 million of cash from operations, less $13 million of CapEx.
  • In our national security portfolio, which includes our Defense and Intelligence businesses, the shutdown caused approximately $60 million in billable expenses to move from our Q3 into Q4.

Cash flow rounded out the picture on a firmer note, backed by solid collections. Still, the government shutdown left its mark. When billables slide from one period to another, liquidity follows. Nothing here signals stress, but it’s a clear reminder that external disruptions can ripple straight through timing.

  • We were also recently awarded nearly $100 million of fixed price work to expand Thunderdome across the Department of War.

From a strategy standpoint, management's putting more emphasis on outcome-based models, which marks an important shift. And if they get it right, the payoff is a structurally better business model. One concrete example cited on the call was the very Back to the Future sounding "Vellox Reverser."

In Rozanski's view:

"Vellox Reverser accelerates an organization's response to today's most complex cyber threats. It performs fully automated malware analysis and delivers actionable intelligence in minutes versus what traditionally takes days. As AI-enhanced cyber attacks increase and become one of the primary threats of 2026, this product is a force multiplier for cyber defense efforts."

  • Booz Allen has committed to deploy up to $400 million in a16z's late-stage venture fund over the life of the fund.

Zooming out, the growth priorities seem fairly evident to me. By focusing more on cyber, AI, national security, and deep tech partnerships, Booz Allen's positioning itself where federal spending is most likely to go. And we know that those investments line tremendously with renewed focus on defense technology and industrial rebuilding that follows the President Donald Trump era playbook. The alliance with a16z (aka Andreessen Horowitz) makes that strategy tangible, and, probably, "good for the country” too.

Booz Allen's Valuation

BAH trades at an EV/EBITDA of 11.55x trailing, a 22% discount to the industrials sector median of 14.81. On a forward basis, it's at about 12.15x versus 12.9x for peers. So only around a 6% discount. I’d argue the stock looks relatively cheap right now because revenue’s been choppy, mostly due to funding delays and shutdowns.

That said, the risks could be viewed as fairly significant, hence yesterday's reaction via any increased oversight like the one involving the U.S. Treasury which would weaken backlog stability and open the door to stronger competition from more agile national security competitors.

Some of you might not be too happy with the balance sheet. Debt makes up about 78% of the company’s capital (per FG above). Meanwhile, silver and gold's been going ballistic, signaling what could be a government shutdown. Even with the doors open, any kind of rising budget pressure could still pinch cash flow. More weakening in civil recovery wouldn't help either.

Still, Booz Allen’s roughly 8% expected annual return suggests the business can absorb some pressure, assuming the backlog converts the way management says it will.

Final Takeaway

All things considered, I'm going with a Hold rating with a slight lean to Buy on weakness. If you're a long-term investor comfortable with federal procurement risk who wants discounted exposure to AI/cyber and national security at ~11–12x EV/EBITDA, I'd start to dip my toes in the water here.

data.title: Tractor Supply: Good Growth Prospects At Reasonable Valuation (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Tractor Supply Company (TSCO) has good growth prospects ahead, supported by its resilient C.U.E. product mix, strong execution across Neighbor’s Club, digital and omnichannel initiatives, scaling of growth platforms such as direct sales and pet prescriptions. As the company's strategic initiatives and its store expansion continue, it is well positioned to see good growth despite subdued consumer environment.

The company's margin prospects are also good, with the operating expense pressure seen in FY25 from strategic investments expected to normalize. I believe the company's P/E valuation multiple should re-rate higher as its growth accelerates. P/E multiple re-rating coupled with good earnings growth and approximately 1.7% dividend yield should result in a good upside. Hence, I am moving to a Buy rating on the stock.

Recent Financial Performance

I last covered TSCO stock in Jul 2025 with a neutral rating. The stock has declined slightly since then, underperforming S&P 500's (SPY) more than 10% gains. While the strategic growth investments by the company have helped its growth since then, they have negatively impacted margins. The company reported 7.2% year-over-year growth in Q3 2025. Its comparable sales increased by 3.9% year-over-year, and new store openings and Allivet acquisition contributed the rest. The comparable average transaction count was up 2.7% and the comparable average ticket increased by 1.2% as the company saw strong demand for seasonal products as well as continued momentum in core categories, especially consumable, usable and edible products.

In terms of margins, gross margin improved by 15 bps year-over-year to 37.4%. The company’s focus on product cost management, selective pricing increases, and successful execution of every day low pricing strategy helped offset higher tariffs and transportation cost. However, operating margin was down 14 bps year-over-year to 9.2% due to investment in initiatives such as direct sales and final mile delivery, increased incentive compensation, and lower benefit from sale-leaseback strategy.

Outlook

Looking ahead, I am optimistic about TSCO’s revenue growth prospects. A large portion of the company’s sales comes from consumable, usable and edible (C.U.E.) categories, which include livestock and pet feed, animal health products, propane and essential maintenance supplies. These products are needs-based rather than discretionary and are sticking up well despite of inflationary environment. C.U.E. categories represent around 40-45% of total revenue and delivered mid-single-digit comparable sales growth in every month in Q3 2025 outperforming other categories.

The company is also executing well in terms of its membership program, digital and omnichannel capabilities, and new growth initiatives like direct sales.

The company’s Neighbor’s Club program now accounts for over 80% of total company sales, and management noted continued gains in both spend per member and retention. The company has implemented a new customer data platform that enables improved personalization and targeted promotion. This should allow Tractor Supply to drive incremental growth through more effective merchandising, customer-specific marketing initiatives, and category expansion.

Tractor Supply is also doing a good job in terms of digital and omnichannel capabilities. Digital sales are growing at low double-digit rates, outpacing the broader company’s growth. Nearly 80% of online orders at TSCO are fulfilled by stores, which lower last-mile costs, improve delivery speed, and leverage the existing store network as a competitive advantage. I believe the company’s final-mile infrastructure should complement the physical store network and improve customer convenience and sales.

Further, the company’s newer growth initiatives are also scaling well. The company’s direct sales program now covers more than 300 stores and the business is generating over $200,000 in weekly sales, with an average ticket size seven times the company average. Management expects this initiative to become self-funding in 2026, which should help margins as well.

Another long-term growth opportunity for the company is pet and animal prescriptions, driven by the Allivet acquisition. On the last earnings call, management noted continued steady growth in pet pharmacy orders and increasing adoption of prescription subscriptions within the Neighbor’s Club ecosystem.

The company also has good store growth opportunity. Tractor Supply opened 29 new stores in Q3, and management noted that the new store continued to perform well in terms of productivity. For FY26, management plans to open approximately 100 stores, versus 90 in 2025. I expect store growth to continue being a good road driver moving forward as well.

I am also optimistic about the company's margin growth prospects. In recent years, the company's operating margins were impacted by strategic investments across areas such as direct sales, final mile delivery, digital infrastructure, and new growth initiatives. These impacted the company's operating margins by 15-20 bps in 2025. However, as these initiatives mature, the company should see less headwind moving forward. For example, management has indicated that the Direct Sales Initiative is expected to become self-funding in 2026.

Overall, the company is nearing the end of its peak investment cycle as a percentage of sales. Management has indicated that 2026 is expected to be a more normalized year in terms of these expenses and margins. Given the revenue outlook is positive, as the operating expense normalizes, I believe that the company can see good leverage.

The company's gross margins have performed well despite tariff-related pressure thanks to its targeted price increases. Also, a large portion of the C.U.E. category is domestically sourced, which limits the company's exposure to tariffs. So, even in case of any adverse development on the tariff front, I expect the company to see good margin performance.

Q4 Earnings And Winter Storm

TSCO is set to report its Q4 earnings on January 29. In addition to long-term growth initiatives, the company's results are also impacted by weather. While Q4 was relatively warmer, the U.S. is currently under a very severe winter snowstorm. This was widely forecasted, and I believe consumers would have rushed to stores to stock up on shovels, road salt, generator, stoves, kerosene, etc., to prepare for it. Since the company is reporting its earnings so close to this winter storm, I believe we should see a lot of queries around year-to-date trends from analysts and a positive commentary by the company on how Q1 is trending. This should help the stock performance going into the earnings and post-earnings. Other than near-term trends, any commentary around continued progress on long-term initiatives should also help the stock.

Valuation

TSCO stock is trading at 23.73x FY26 consensus EPS estimates of $2.30 and 21.40x FY27 consensus EPS estimates of $2.56 per share. This is a discount vs its 5-year average forward P/E of 24.1x.

I believe this lower than historical valuation is a result of slower earnings growth seen in FY25 where the company's profitability was pressured by strategic investments in areas such as direct sales, final mile delivery, digital infrastructure, and new growth initiatives.

As discussed earlier, management has indicated that these growth investments as a percentage of sales are near their peak and key initiatives such as direct sales are expected to enter a self-funding phase starting 2026. This should result in more normalized expenses and margins in FY26 and beyond. If we look at consensus estimates, EPS growth is expected to accelerate back to double-digit in FY26 and FY27, compared to low single-digit EPS growth in FY25. I believe this earnings growth acceleration should result in a P/E multiple re-rating.

The P/E multiple re-rating coupled with double-digit earnings growth should result in a good upside for the stock. In addition, TSCO stock also offers approximately 1.7% dividend yield, which should further add to TSCO stock.

Management has been executing well. While the company's growth is currently getting impacted by a subdued consumer environment, this is unlikely to persist forever. Any improvement in broader macros and consumer confidence can improve investors' sentiment towards retail stocks, which could act as an additional tailwind for growth and valuation. Overall, I find risk-reward attractive and rate TSCO stock a buy.

Risks

The biggest risk for the stock is macro-related. While I am expecting macro to remain stable or even improve slightly, if the inflation accelerates or rural spending takes another leg down, the company's traffic may see some pressure.

I am expecting continued strong execution on key growth initiatives like digital and Omnichannel Capabilities, direct sales, final-mile delivery and pet prescription. However, if there are any execution related issues that impact the traction on these initiatives or there is any delay in these initiatives reaching self-funding phase, it might impact results.

data.title: Amid The Chaos, The Energy Sector Is Sending A Message
data.description:
Amid the chaotic headlines both domestic and abroad, the stock market continued to grind higher yesterday. Not even the haunting message that soaring gold and silver prices are supposedly sending could halt the uptrend. Meanwhile, the dollar fell to its lowest level since 2022, and energy prices rose, led by a surge in natural gas in response to the brutal cold weather that blanketed the country over the weekend. Market pundits are raising concerns about the parabolic moves in precious metals, as though the price increases reflect rising fears of currency debasement, inflation, and geopolitical instability, yet I have a different take.

Gold prices (GLD) are clearly impacted by a weakening dollar and increased demand from central banks. The structural imbalance between supply and demand in the silver market warrants higher prices as well, but it appears to me that the surge in both in recent weeks is more a function of huge speculative retail investment flows by investors who have never owned the metals before. This is why silver (SLV) soared more than 14% yesterday in its biggest one-day move since the Global Financial Crisis in 2008 before plunging 12% into the close. These metals are no longer the safe havens they were before.

I have always maintained exposure to both as core holdings in my all-weather investment strategy, but this is for the purpose of diversification into assets that were non-correlated with the stock market. They have now become meme stocks. Granted, silver is an industrial metal that is supply constrained and in high demand for use in solar panels, electric vehicles, and now AI data centers, but these trends have been in place for a long time. Did that demand suddenly surge 3x in three weeks? The only explanation for these recent moves is speculation, which I think will end badly for investors who are looking to jump on the bandwagon for a quick trade. If I had to guess, I think that meme stock investors who emerged in 2021 are now piling into precious metals, which is one reason cryptocurrencies have lost their luster this year. Perhaps these prices will hold, but forecasting the price of silver in particular over the next 12 months is like trying to place a target on Bitcoin.

A largely unnoticed development is going on the energy sector (XLE), and it spells a more bullish message for the market. The percentage of stocks in the energy sector that are at 52-week highs has risen above 31% for the first time in over 300 days. Why is that relevant? Historically, while the near-term results are mixed, the S&P 500 has been higher six, nine, and 12 months later every single time. The batting average is not bad for the three-month period that follows either.

Why the interest in energy stocks? The reasoning has been different every time this occurred, but what it suggests in general is stronger rates of global growth, as cyclicals begin to outperform. This resonates with my market and economic outlooks at the beginning of this year. It may run contrary to the ominous headlines we have been seeing in the news since the beginning of the year, but the market is forward looking. Strengthening the tailwind from the rising percentage of 52-week highs is the fact that 95% of the stocks in the energy sector are now above their 200-day moving average for the first time in a year. The historical returns for the S&P 500 that followed this development are just as impressive.

We have earnings reports from four of the Magnificent 7 this week, but I am more interested in what the remaining 493 produce, as well as the small- and mid-cap names that have been leading the broad market in performance this year. I expect valuations to hold for the technology behemoths, while they expand for the rest of the market, driven by a broad-based improvement in earnings growth. This rotation in performance leadership is the most important investment theme for 2026.

data.title: Fed Rate Cuts On Hold Till June, According To Market Forecasts
data.description:
The Federal Reserve is expected to leave its target rate unchanged at tomorrow’s policy meeting, marking a shift after three straight cuts in 2025. Keeping rates steady is the current outlook until June, but several factors are in play that could change the calculus in the weeks ahead.

Let’s start with what appears likely: the Fed funds futures market is pricing in a high probability that the central bank will keep its target rate steady at a 3.5-3.75% range at tomorrow’s FOMC meeting.

The policy-sensitive 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) is aligned with the no-change outlook. The difference between this widely followed yield and the current effective Fed funds (EFF) rate is close to the smallest spread in nearly a year. That’s a sign that this slice of the Treasury market is no longer anticipating rate cuts, which was the case in previous months, when the 2-year yield traded well below EFF – falling below -80 basis points at one point last May.

As always, the outlook turns increasingly cloudy the further beyond the next FOMC meeting we look, but a host of conflicting factors make looking ahead unusually challenging.

One source for thinking that the Fed will be hard pressed to cut rates anytime soon: US economic activity has remained resilient, which suggests that easing policy further is unnecessary if not inflationary.

Although some economists last year warned that recession risk was rising, recent nowcasts for the upcoming (and delayed) fourth quarter GDP report are pointing to ongoing strength. The Atlanta Fed’s GDPNow model is currently nowcasting that Q4 growth will accelerate to 5.4%, which (if correct) would mark the highest output in four years.

Analysts warn that developing relatively reliable Q4 nowcasts is still challenging because of the lingering effects from the government shutdown in October. But as The Capital Spectator reported last week, taking a more cautious view of Q4 nowcasts, by using a median estimate from several sources, still points to a relatively robust, if slower, economy in the final three months of 2025. On that basis, there’s arguably still a case for going slow with policy changes, if not pausing rate cuts.

Peter Hooper, vice chair of research at Deutsche Bank, agrees. “It’s time to sit back and take a look at things,” he tells The New York Times. “We will get some further easing, but it’s not urgent at this point.”

The counterview is that inflation, for all the concern surrounding a tariff-driven shock, remains relatively stable, which in turn leaves room for more rate cuts, if only as insurance to keep the economy humming. Last week’s delayed update of November’s personal consumption expenditures price index – the Fed’s preferred measure of inflation – ticked up to a 2.8% year-over-year pace for headline and core readings. That’s up from 2.7% in October and slightly further above the Fed’s 2% target.

The Richmond Fed’s analysis of the latest monthly PCE numbers in context with the historical record, however, suggests that “inflation is behaving as it did prior to the pandemic, in a manner broadly consistent with — or slightly below — the Fed’s 2 percent target.”

Meanwhile, the jury’s still out on whether the recent updates showing a slowdown in hiring are a sign of a broader economic slowdown in progress. Some economists say that the marked downshift in payrolls growth warrants more rate cuts. The pushback is that softer hiring has yet to translate to a rise in layoffs, as indicated by the low level of jobless claims in recent months.

Adding to the Fed’s challenge of setting rates in a complex macro environment is the ongoing political pressure from the White House to ease policy further. President Trump last week renewed his criticism of Fed Chair Jerome Powell and told CNBC that inflation had been “defeated.”

Perhaps the Fed’s biggest challenge is persuading markets that, whatever the central bank decides in the months ahead, it’s setting rates based on independent analysis of the economy. That’s getting harder at a time of an ongoing criminal investigation of Fed Governor Lisa Cook, which some observers say is a thinly-veiled effort by the White House to influence the central bank’s decisions in favor of more rate cuts by opening up a new seat at the Fed.

Further roiling the outlook: Powell’s tenure as Fed chair ends in May, and it’s unclear how a new Fed chair would influence policy.

So far, however, the Treasury market appears relatively calm, and so it can be argued that the crowd isn’t particularly worried. The benchmark 10-year yield (US10Y), although it’s increased lately to the highest level since August a few days ago, is still trading at a middling range vs. recent history.

A rise in this key yield above its recent trading range could change the calculus, perhaps dramatically. All the more so at a time of growing concern about government debt around the world.

The rise of gold above $5000 an ounce this week highlights the growing concerns related to the so-called debasement.

“We’re at the start of a global debt crisis, with markets increasingly fearful governments will attempt to inflate away out-of-control debt,” wrote Robin Brooks, a senior fellow at the Brookings Institution and former chief economist at the Institute of International Finance.

For now, at least, the Treasury market has yet to signal trouble ahead in the form of persistently rising yields. When and if that changes, the risk calculus will change. The challenge of the moment is deciding if this is the calm before the storm, or just another round of noise driven by overbaked warnings.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Boeing Beats On Orders, Airbus Wins Deliveries: Why Winning Still Feels Like Losing
data.description:
Boeing (NYSE:BA) and Airbus (EADSF) have both reported their full year airplane order and delivery numbers. As expected, Airbus led the airplane deliveries leaderboard while Boeing ended up in the top spot in terms of airplane orders. While both jet makers may consider their lead in the respective categories a win, I believe there is a lot of context to be provided that shows that the wins were not as impressive as one might think.

In this report, I analyze the airplane orders and deliveries for 2025. As mentioned in prior analyses, we're uniquely positioned to provided value estimates on orders and deliveries to more closely reflect the actual benefit to the businesses.

Below, I have attached the explanation of the analysis methodology from a prior report:

Explanation Of Analysis Methodology For Airplane Orders And Deliveries

For this analysis, I use an internally developed tool available on The Aerospace Forum. I will be assessing the net orders and deliveries. For each, I will discuss the units ordered and delivered and the associated dollar value.

The net orders provide us with a view of demand. This is a reflection of factors such as pricing and availability. Boeing and Airbus have a total backlog of nearly 15,000 airplanes. So, currently, we're not viewing things through the scope of needing more orders to support or increase production. The fact is that the backlogs have to be brought down to healthier levels, and that can be achieved through higher deliveries. Deliveries are what matters, and that's also the area where we see that neither Boeing nor Airbus has been able to support demand. Assessing the deliveries gives us a view of how the recovery for both manufacturers is pacing.

Boeing Expands Leads On Airbus In Airplane Order Race

In some way, the net orders show something that is not completely unsurprising. Airbus booked most net orders for single aisle airplanes while Boeing booked most for wide body and freight airplanes. Also interesting to note is that Airbus received 44 net orders for the Airbus A220, which is a non-native Airbus airplane for which Boeing has no competitive answer. So, two thirds of Airbus’ lead in the single aisle category was driven by the A220 which is the rebranded C-Series that Boeing pushed into Airbus’ hands by filing a dispute against Bombardier, the designer of the jet, years ago which led to Airbus acquiring the airplane program. In total, Boeing won 1,075 net orders compared to 889 for Airbus giving Boeing the lead.

Single aisle airplanes come at a substantially lower price tag than wide body jets and that also means that in terms of value Boeing had the upper hand. Airbus led by $5.1 billion in the single aisle category, but Boeing ended the year with $43.8 billion more in orders bringing the company a $38.7 billion dollar value lead in orders.

Airbus And Boeing Book Net Airplane Orders Valued At Over $190 Billion

The image above shows where things actually get interesting. We see that Boeing and Airbus had a combined order inflow of 1,9647 jets which was a 63% year-on-year increase. However, we see that Airbus orders increased 8% while Boeing net order inflow grew 185%. It gave Boeing a 55% share in the net orders. In terms of value, Boeing and Airbus received $190.5 billion in net orders marking a 91% increase. Airbus net order value increased 14% while Boeing’s net order value surged almost 250% giving it a 60% share in the net order value. We note that 2023 was an outlier in terms of net orders as Indian airlines placed substantial orders during the year. Both jet makers saw order value rise faster than net orders and that is driven by a higher share of wide body airplanes in the order mix.

The interesting part is the graph above. The graph shows Airbus’ order net order lead over the years and it shows that the company’s lead shot up to over 750 units due to the Boeing 737 MAX crash and the subsequent grounding. Then during the grounding and the pandemic, the net order lead rose to around 1,500 units and increased further to nearly 2,300 units in 2023 driven by timing of big orders from Indian airlines and then a further increase to 2,750 units in 2024 as Boeing dealt with the aftermath of the MAX 9 incident. In 2025, the company only reduced the deficit with Airbus by 186 orders and Airbus’ cumulated lead since 2019 still is north of 2,500. The graph above definitely is not the only graph you could take to show the self-inflicted pain of Boeing, but it is a telling one.

Airbus Won Delivery Race On Both Metrics

Looking at the deliveries, which is in the end all that really matters to generate cash. Airbus ended up with 253 single aisle deliveries more than Boeing, but Boeing ended up with 60 deliveries more in the wide body category. So, Airbus had a 193 unit lead over Boeing. Even though single aisle airplanes are substantially cheaper compared to wide body airplanes, the lead that Airbus built in the single aisle airplane category was too big for Boeing to offset it through wide body deliveries. Airbus had $13.7 billion more in single aisle airplane deliveries, partially offset by a $8.7 billion lead for Boeing in wide body and freighter deliveries.

Once again, it is interesting to evaluate the impact of the Airbus A220 on the numbers. Airbus delivered 93 A220s in 2025 which account for half of the delivery gap with Boeing. In terms of value this is around $3.7 billion. So, absent of these deliveries Airbus would have a $1.3 billion lead over Boeing. In my view that shows the value that the Airbus A220 adds for Airbus. At the same time, it also puts on display that the on the programs where both jet makers compete Airbus has not been that dominant. The jet maker has been pushing for higher production rates on the A320neo and A350 programs at a time when Boeing was struggling on both programs but if we look at the numbers, we see that Airbus has not been able to blow Boeing away as it once intended. That is fully driven by supply chain issues. Airbus had set rates and targets that could simply not be supported by the supply chain.

Airbus And Boeing Airplane Deliveries Exceed 2019 Levels

Looking at the combined deliveries, we see that Boeing and Airbus delivered nearly 1,400 airplanes which marked the highest level over the past six years. At the same time, we note that Airbus deliveries declined by nearly 70 units compared to 2019 while Boeing deliveries increased by 220 units. Airbus remained dominant with a 67% share in deliveries, but if there is one conclusion that we can draw safely is that the jet maker did not out of the pandemic in a strong fashion. Especially since 2024, we see that the delivery performance has been underwhelming. Boeing hasn’t been doing great either but it looks like its approach towards supply chain management is somewhat better, but this is also partially driven by the benefit the company had from an inventory of pre-built jets.

That neither jet maker is impressing with the delivery performance can also be seen when we just try to fit a linear trend from 2020. Boeing deliveries should have hit around 815 in 2025 and that means that the company is falling behind the recovery curve by almost 560 airplanes. Airbus is behind the recovery curve by almost 65 units and that is an interesting observation with the knowledge that during the second half of 2025 the European jet maker had built an inventory of 60 airplanes that were awaiting engine deliveries for customer hand-over. So, Boeing is still behind a lot and Airbus’ is trailing at a much more acceptable level in comparison but we note that both jet maker are trailing on the recovery curve and that is not a curve that counts the difference between the targeted production rates pre-pandemic and the actual delivery performance because that gap would likely run in the thousands of airplanes.

In terms of value Boeing and Airbus delivered $96.2 billion worth of jets with a 53% share for Airbus and a 47% share for Boeing. Interesting to note is that Boeing is now exceeding the 2019 levels driven by an easier comp, but Airbus is roughly $10 billion behind.

Conclusion: Both Jet Makers Struggle In One Way Or Another

On paper, Boeing won in terms of net orders in 2025. However, we note that even after the 2025 win, the company has lacked behind on orders. Airbus has booked over 2,500 more orders since 2019 which shows how the problems with Boeing airplanes has affected order inflow. Looking at the deliveries, we see that unsurprisingly Airbus ended up on top but half of the company’s lead over Boeing comes from the non-native A220 and the company has actually failed to wipe out Boeing in terms of deliveries. It is highly likely that if Boeing would not have the incident with the Boeing 737 MAX in 2024 and the work stoppage in the second half of the year, today’s delivery numbers would have looked substantially better.

In my view it shows the realities of a jet maker that landed itself into a crisis and its competitor that failed miserably to ramp up production to hit Boeing at a time it was weak.

I believe that both jet maker have appeal for a buy rating as demand for airplanes has shown to be robust and both jet makers have backlogs that support growth. The challenges that both jet makers are currently facing is to align the supply chain and overall production system with envisioned targets in line with demand. In that regard, 2026 will be an interesting year as we will see whether Airbus can better align the supply chain to meet delivery goals and for Boeing we will see whether it can hike production on the Boeing 737 MAX and Boeing 787 programs with little to no cushion in the delivery figures from pre-built airplanes that are delivered.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Nebius: The Q4 Results Should Trigger A Rally (Earnings Preview)
data.description:
My Coverage History And Thesis Update On Nebius

I'm glad to be one of the first analysts on Seeking Alpha who laid out a bullish thesis on Nebius Group N.V. (NBIS) when the stock price was trading at $36/share and then dropped to ~$22/share in April 2025. I trimmed my large position close to $50-52/share after the first doubling, as I was heavily buying the massive dip at low-20s, getting overexposed to NBIS at some point. The trimming was stress-relieving, but it's almost physically painful to imagine the opportunity cost that I incurred along with it.

Anyway, I want to move on. My past three articles on NBIS compared the firm with CoreWeave (CRWV) and IREN (IREN), and the latest two articles were more bullish on NBIS than on any of its peers. Today, my preview article is going to be dedicated mainly to the upcoming Q4 earnings on February 13. The company is reporting in less than 3 weeks, and I see no articles discussing about Nebius Group's Q4 results specifically, so let me pioneer this topic of the season.

From what I see, the current consensus expectations are still pricing slightly more conservatively than what management guided for, because the firm has clearly been just a vision-driven "belief" story for investors. And the revenue beating track record isn't the one any public company would envy - the difficulty in properly setting the revenue targets based on the guided ARRs took its toll.

Still, I think Nebius is getting closer to its execution-driven model, where delivery speed dictates revenues. I think the management will give us some updates on Nebius' Finnish data center phases, which were scheduled for December, and the launch of the New Jersey and London facilities. Any miss on the top or bottom line shouldn't matter that much, in my view - it's about operational updates that should drive the stock's price action. Since I have a high conviction in the management team and their ability to move forward quickly, I think the Q4 updates will be bullish, and they'll be able to push the stock price back to its previous local highs. I keep my Buy in place ahead of the Q4 print.

Previewing Nebius Group's Q4 Earnings

In Nebius' Q3 earnings update, the management team decided to tighten the FY2025 revenue guidance to $500-550 million so that the midpoint becomes "clearer," so to speak. In other words, the full-year revenue midpoint is now at $525 million, while the previous guidance was pointing at $450-630 million with the midpoint at $540 million. A narrower range was actually a good thing, in my opinion, because it gave us much more clarity. But the market punished NBIS with a 7%+ sell-off right after the print, and NBIS stayed under some pressure throughout the whole calendar Q4.

The intentional cutting of the higher end of the initial guidance was a smart move, in my opinion, as it freed up some room for possible miss. Now, if NBIS delivers $540 million like it was forecasted initially, that would be a beat of ~2.8% to the guidance midpoint. They don't need to deliver close to $690 million to beat. However, the problem with the management's midpoint guidance this time is that the market is expecting NBIS to reach a slightly higher revenue in Q4 versus the updated high-end prediction:

I think they're likely to beat that high-end anyway. Why?

Throughout the previous earnings call, the management noted that the core infrastructure's revenue was capped by the capacity limits and timing of additional capacity going online, not the demand environment. If so, then the upcoming Q4 data will be essential because we'll have updates on NBIS's success in Finland and New Jersey (plus the London facility, which was added in November).

And we are growing with the numbers of the regions where we are present. And we're also bringing new capacity online in the current sites. We're also coming online in New Jersey. We are launching new phases of Finland in Q4, which are also presold by the way.

Source: NBIS's Q3 earnings call, Andrey Korolenko's comments

I think the facility they launched in London in early November will drive up their revenues to an extent that analysts failed to account for. Why? Well, management didn't mention it on the earnings call, and analysts didn't bother to ask questions about that specific facility, so I assume it's a net addition to the revenue side in Q4.

I saw some commentary on X and elsewhere, with some analysts expecting Microsoft's (MSFT) deal and the contract with Meta (META) to influence the upcoming Q4 revenue in a positive way. But it's misleading to me because both deals are back-weighted, so NBIS will likely see its revenue surge in late 2026. However, I don't rule out the significance of these deals when it comes to driving the narrative. We saw how new deals additions have impacted the management's views before - see the Q3 earnings call and the CEO's words on regarding the ARR long-term trends.

And we have a large pipeline of new software and services that we are continuing to build, which will differentiate us from other cloud companies. Based on the strength in demand that we see and our accelerated capacity growth plan, we believe we can achieve annualized run rate revenue ARR of $7 billion to $9 billion by the end of 2026.

So, if the management comes out with yet another update regarding the volume rise or ARR boost, the stock price will likely see a massively positive reaction right after such commentaries.

NBIS: Valuation Update

I recall that when I called for trimming NBIS in June 2025, its FY2026 P/S ratio was slightly over 8x. Now it's less than 6.7x, with a forecasted slump below 3x by the end of 2027, based on the updated consensus estimates (YCharts data below).

There's also one interesting thing: Two Wall Street analysts who form the consensus for FY2027 predict an EPS range of -$1.28 to $6.00, with a midpoint of $2.36, based on Seeking Alpha. But then, for FY2028, we see a complete absence of diversity of opinion - there's only one analyst forming the consensus.

It tells me that NBIS has a massive opportunity for repricing in terms of its EPS because a) the management seeks to run operations profitable (we remember that from the CEO's commentary regarding EBITDA breakeven), and b) as the new capacity finally kicks in and starts to generate revenues, and more analysts assess that, the consensus will naturally move to the upside.

With all that in mind, I think the fair price-to-sales multiple for NBIS - if it breaks even on the adjusted EBITDA front and scales profitably on that metric - should be close to 6-8x by the end of 2027. With that in mind, the implied upside potential, conservatively assuming no changes to the current FY2027 sales consensus, arrives at a $46.68 billion market cap. The stock might double over the next 1-2 years if it follows the above path I've just described.

The Bottom Line

The key risk to my bullish update lies in NBIS's execution and the management's ability to deliver on time. There are a lot of moving parts in the neocloud sector, and everything that makes it run, with plenty of partners, suppliers, and market conditions that Nebius Group can't control. Another risk is that in the case of another miss, NBIS stock could drop heavily, as was the case after Q4 2024 or Q3 2025. Plus, I noticed that many ordinary investors have become interested in this neocloud name, and if they overreact to a miss, they might decide to bail out and create a massive supply of NBIS stock, thus pressuring the price.

Despite all that, I believe the upcoming quarterly release and subsequent updates from the management team might spark another rally if execution at the Finnish, New Jersey, and London facilities goes as planned (or even ahead of schedule). I haven't covered non-core business lines today because they're relatively small and don't contribute much to NBIS's neocloud story. But who knows, the non-core assets can surprise us to the upside as well. At least I saw that Clickhouse raised a new financing round at a higher valuation mark. We shall see.

For now, I think NBIS should have a Buy rating. Let me know what you think of NBIS stock ahead of its Q4 earnings release in the comment section below.

Thank you for reading!

data.title: IREN: Why I'm Still Bullish Heading Into Earnings
data.description:
Investment Thesis

While I've been bullish on IREN Limited (IREN) from the very start, this is the first time we're seeing a shift in the market’s engagement with the story. As the stock is up 16% since my last coverage, it is no longer valued on the narrative or the AI optionality, it is valued as a forward-deployed infrastructure play where execution is key.

Note that I am not anchoring this thesis on reported revenues but rather on contracted ARR that converts to its underlying form. The economics are already visible via the dated contracts, including the Microsoft (MSFT) one, which implies significant annualized revenues when power is activated. The issue isn’t demand, it’s the sequencing of power availability.

This call isn’t about beating revenues or EPS. It’s a confirmation call to ensure contracted ARR continues to convert from reserved to active. If the conversion timelines hold up, the income statement is simply lagging the truth, and the thesis remains firmly intact.

ARR First, Revenue Later: Why the Income Statement Is Lagging Reality

If we consider the case of IREN, we don’t anchor based on revenue figures that they report, but rather anchor based on the emerging ARR, which is actually reflected in the Microsoft contract that implies up to approximately $1.9 billion in annualized revenue potential once fully phased in and power is activated.

And this is where investors might mistakenly interpret the income statement. ARR is increasing faster than it is recognized. The ramp is controlled by power availability and deployment sequence, rather than by demand. Thus, revenue is recognized after the underlying economic reality is built beneath the surface.

What I am focusing on in the coming call is not the headline ARR growth, but rather the degree to which contracted ARR is turning into its underlying form. The level of reserved capacity, the level of capacity that’s scheduled, the level of capacity that’s already monetizable, these numbers matter much more than the quarter-to-quarter growth in revenues. If the company can make the case that the ARR is essentially locked in, it’s just waiting on the power to be turned on, the market will continue to ignore the noise.

Connected Power vs. Active Power: What is the Real KPI?

One of the biggest distinctions in this story, and what I think sets sophisticated investors from casual ones, is the distinction between connected power and active power. IREN was very transparent in terms of the fact that it had control over a large power portfolio. However, control alone is not what creates revenue. Revenue is created only when the megawatts are actually plugged in, or energized, or connected.

Currently, a large chunk of IREN’s strength is either under construction or awaiting energization. The company’s Sweetwater 1 alone represents ~1.4GW in gross load that is either under construction or awaiting energization and is still guided for April 2026. This earnings call is at a critical juncture at which investors are looking to be reassured that the timelines remain in place and the construction risks are well managed.

New megawatts? No thanks. I just need the management to reinforce the message that the existing megawatts on the pipeline continue to move from connected to active as planned. Any evidence of delays/slowdowns on the grid will disproportionately impact the Street as it directly impacts the achievement of the ARR.

However, should management be able to strengthen its assertion of a connected power gradually transitioning to active, revenue-producing power, then it is safe to say that such a thesis stands on firm ground. Power conversion is a bottleneck, demand is not.

Earnings Preview: When Valuation Prices the Back Half

Heading into Q2 FY26, consensus sits at a level of $227.33 million in revenues, along with a loss of -$0.09 EPS, which will be reported on Feb 5, after market. On the surface, it appears to be a lackluster quarter for a company whose stock is currently trading for $56.68, particularly given a D+ grade for its valuation and a forward P/S ratio of 16.21x its FY26 revenues. But, of course, it also does not address what those numbers really mean.

First, revenue growth is not slowing. While the revenue is only $227 million, consensus growth is approximately 90% YoY, which is followed by $280.9 million in Q3, or 89.7% YoY, and $390.9 million in Q4, or 107% YoY. It is not reasonable to expect slowing growth like this and interpret it as demand fatigue as it looks more like capacity gating. Consensus is modeling a big step-up in the back half of the year, and it only makes sense if the power is coming on schedule.

But that’s EPS, where investors get confused. EPS is estimated at $1.05 for FY26, which works out to a forward 53.9x GAAP P/E, which improves marginally to 53.5x in FY27, even though the topline increases by $1.59 billion, or 138% ($1.15 billion to $2.74 billion). The market is not pricing near-term operating leverage, only asset activation followed by margin improvement, as depreciation, SBC, and pre-revenue costs remain front-loaded.

Priced for Success, Tested on Execution

The risk isn’t demand. The risk is execution concentration. There’s a lot of future ARR associated with a small number of hyperscale customers, all with a very sequenced rollout plan. There’s a fragile equilibrium where everything’s working as long as the timelines work.

If the energization process slips or if deployments are delayed or if capital costs escalate unexpectedly, the market’s reaction is almost instant. The market doesn’t wait to be told why something has not worked out. The market de-risks first and then re-evaluates later. This is the cost of being priced-for-success.

This is why I think the upcoming earnings is really a trust check. Investors aren’t asking the management team to prove the vision again. They’re actually asking the management team to prove that the machine is still working exactly as it was last quarter.

Takeaway

However, the upcoming earnings is not about IREN beating or missing the revenue and/or EPS estimates. It is a confirmation call on the execution side. Investors would be looking to be reassured on the progress and confirmation on the power activation, sequencing, and construction timetables, especially on the Sweetwater side.

data.title: Energy Transfer And Capital Spending Shifts: Implications For MLP Cash Flows
data.description:
Midstream investors are once again paying close attention to capital budgets. After several years of prioritizing payouts and balance sheet repair, large pipeline operators are beginning to diverge in how aggressively they reinvest. Some are leaning into new projects, while others are deliberately pulling back. For master limited partnerships, this distinction matters. Capital spending directly influences distributable cash flow, coverage ratios, and ultimately the sustainability of distributions.¹

Across the sector, valuations remain well below historical norms despite relatively stable operating fundamentals.² That disconnect has renewed focus on how individual partnerships balance growth ambitions with cash discipline.

Energy Transfer’s Pipeline Push

Energy Transfer (ET) entered 2026 signaling a renewed emphasis on growth. Management announced plans to allocate roughly $5 to $5.5 billion toward expansion projects, with most of the spending directed toward natural gas infrastructure.³ The strategy reflects expectations for rising demand tied to power generation, data centers, and industrial activity.

To support this shift, the partnership paused development of its proposed Lake Charles LNG export terminal while accelerating investment in pipeline and processing assets that can be placed into service more quickly.⁴ Several of these projects are expected to begin contributing to cash flows during 2026, supporting management’s expectation of improving earnings as volumes ramp.⁵

In the near term, however, higher capital outlays absorb cash that might otherwise support distributions or faster debt reduction. Energy Transfer has emphasized that it intends to fund projects internally, but elevated spending still places pressure on coverage if timelines slip or costs rise.⁶ Investors remain mindful of prior cycles when aggressive capital programs strained cash flows, reinforcing the importance of disciplined execution.

Diverging Paths Across the Midstream Sector

Not all large MLPs are taking the same approach. Enterprise Products Partners (EPD) is moving toward the end of a multiyear expansion cycle. After several years of elevated spending, management expects capital expenditures to decline meaningfully in 2026, freeing up excess cash that can be allocated to distributions, unit repurchases, or balance sheet improvements.⁷

Kinder Morgan (KMI) is pursuing a similarly measured path, with capital plans focused primarily on internally funded expansions rather than large, balance sheet-intensive projects.⁸

For investors, these diverging strategies highlight an important trade-off. Companies increasing capital spending may offer stronger long-term growth, but often at the expense of near-term cash flexibility. Firms scaling back investment tend to generate steadier free cash flow, which can support distribution stability even if growth moderates.

What It Means for Cash Flows and Valuations

Despite solid fundamentals, many MLPs continue to trade below historical valuation ranges.² Capital allocation decisions help explain why. Elevated spending can delay the realization of cash returns, even when underlying assets perform well. Conversely, restrained budgets can quickly improve coverage metrics and reinforce investor confidence.

Capital spending is not merely an accounting detail. It is the primary lever that determines how much cash ultimately reaches unitholders. Investors evaluating midstream partnerships should weigh not just headline income but also where each company sits in its investment cycle. The most compelling opportunities often emerge when disciplined capital plans align with durable, contract-backed cash flows.

Footnotes

  1. Reuters, “Energy Transfer expects to spend up to $5.5 billion in 2026 on natgas network,” January 6, 2026.

  2. Geoffrey Seiler, “Energy Transfer vs. Enterprise Products Partners: Which High-Yield Pipeline Stock Will Outperform in 2026?” Nasdaq, December 14, 2025.

  3. Reuters, “Energy Transfer expects to spend up to $5.5 billion in 2026 on natgas network,” January 6, 2026.

  4. Matt DiLallo, “Every Energy Transfer Investor Should Keep an Eye on This Number,” The Motley Fool, June 13, 2025.

  5. “Energy Transfer Expects to Stomp on the Gas in 2026,” Nasdaq, December 18, 2025.

  6. Matt DiLallo, “Every Energy Transfer Investor Should Keep an Eye on This Number,” Nasdaq, June 13, 2025.

  7. Matt DiLallo, “The First Energy Stock I Plan to Buy in 2026,” The Motley Fool, January 4, 2026.

  8. Kinder Morgan, “Kinder Morgan Announces 2026 Financial Expectations,” Business Wire, December 8, 2025.

Disclosure

This information is not an offer to sell, or solicitation of an offer to buy any investment product, security, or services offered by Jay Hatfield, or Infrastructure Capital Advisors, LLC, (“ICA”) or its affiliates. ICA, will only conduct such solicitation of an offer to buy any investment product or service offered by ICA, if at all, by (1) purported definitive documentation (which will include disclosures relating to investment objective, policies, risk factors, fees, tax implications and relevant qualifications), (2) to qualified participants, if applicable, and (3) only in those jurisdictions where permitted by law.
